figures.caption: Figure 2: Learning curves on 8 DMControl tasks over 5 random seeds using 1 expert trajectory.

Table 1: A summary of the expert sample complexity and interaction complexity. Here H is the horizon length, ε is the desired imitation gap, |S | is the state space size, |A| is the action space size, |Π| is the cardinality of the finite policy class Π, d is the feature dimension, dGEC is the generalized eluder coefficient, N pRhq and N pQhq denote the covering numbers of the reward and Q-value classes. We use rO to hide logarithmic factors.1

Figure 1: Learning curves on 8 DMControl tasks over 5 random seeds using 1 expert trajectory. Here the x-axis is the number of environment interactions and the y-axis is the return. The shaded region corresponds to the standard deviation over 5 random seeds. We run model-based approaches for 200k environment interactions, after which the last return is extended horizontally with dotted lines.
Figure 4: Learning curves on 8 DMControl tasks over 5 random seeds using 7 expert trajectories.
Figure 5: Learning curves on 8 DMControl tasks over 5 random seeds using 10 expert trajectories.

Table 2: Policy returns on 8 DMControl tasks over 5 random seeds following 500k environment interactions for model-free methods or 200k environment interactions for model-based methods. Here ˚ denotes the model-based methods and the remaining are model-free. We use ˘ to denote the standard deviation over 5 random seeds. We highlight both the maximum mean values and values within one standard deviation thereof for model-free and model-based methods, respectively.
Figure 3: Learning curves on 8 DMControl tasks over 5 random seeds using 4 expert trajectories.
Table 3: Pearson correlation between the ground-truth reward and the finally learned reward on 8 DMControl tasks over 5 random seeds.
